evals.prompt: Write HTML for a simple sales table by region and quarter.
evals.expected_output: Uses a caption, column headers with scope="col", and row headers with scope="row".
expectations: The table has a caption
Column headers use scope="col"
Row headers use scope="row"
The table is understandable without relying on visual styling

evals.prompt: Write HTML for a table with two levels of column headers for quarterly revenue.
evals.expected_output: Uses spanned headers with scope="colgroup" and a second row of column headers.
expectations: The table uses colgroup header cells for grouped columns
A second header row labels the individual quarters
The structure can be announced correctly by screen readers
The table remains simple enough to understand

evals.prompt: Create a highly complex staff schedule table that needs explicit header associations.
evals.expected_output: Uses headers and id attributes to map data cells to the correct row and column headers.
expectations: Header cells have stable id values
Data cells reference their headers with headers=
The association is explicit rather than inferred
The structure is only used when scope would be insufficient

evals.prompt: Refactor a layout table used only for page positioning into accessible markup.
evals.expected_output: Replaces the layout table with semantic HTML and CSS layout, rather than keeping a presentational table.
expectations: The solution does not use a table for layout
Semantics are preserved with non-table elements
The visual layout is handled by CSS
The markup remains meaningful when CSS is removed

evals.prompt: Write a budget table that will print across multiple pages and keep the headers visible.
evals.expected_output: Uses caption plus thead and tbody so the table is structurally clear and print-friendly.
expectations: The table has a caption
The table includes a thead section
The table includes a tbody section
The structure supports repeated headers in print
